figures.gloss: The supreme God who sits above the world and orders it but is not approached at the shrines — governance is delegated to his 'children', the khvtishvilni, dispatched to rule the communities of men, with Kviria as their steward and herald.
figures.facet: A Christian-shaped high God administering a feudal pantheon: the highland cosmos mirrors medieval Georgian kingship — sovereign, court, and enfeoffed lords — projected onto heaven.
figures.provenance: Georges Charachidze, 'Le systeme religieux de la Georgie paienne' (1968); Vera Bardavelidze's East Georgian mountaineer fieldwork (1930s-50s); Zurab Kiknadze's jvari-and-saqmo studies.

figures.gloss: The divine beings who actually run the highlands: each community is the saqmo — the 'vassalage' — of its own jvari or khati ('cross' / 'icon'), which owns land, banners, and treasure, punishes impurity, and speaks through its possessed oracle, the kadagi. The words are Orthodoxy's own; the beings they name are local lords of an independent shrine religion.
figures.facet: The host church's vocabulary — cross, icon, saint — captured and re-purposed as the names of resident gods: nominal Christianity as the skin of a distinct system.
figures.provenance: Charachidze (1968); Kiknadze on jvari and saqmo; Kevin Tuite's Khevsur/Pshav studies (1990s-2000s).

figures.gloss: St. George multiplied into a host of local shrine-lords — Lasharis Jvari, the George of Khakhmati, Tetri ('White') Giorgi — the greatest of the khvtishvilni and the highlanders' war-leader among gods. In the Khevsur ballads he leads the raid on Kajaveti, the demon-smiths' land, carrying off their anvil, their treasure, and the kaji-woman Samdzimari, who becomes a shrine power at Khakhmati.
figures.facet: The saint's dragon has faded but the campaigning warrior remains and escalates — George here does not merely slay a monster, he conquers and plunders the otherworld like an epic king.
figures.provenance: Khevsur and Pshav ballad corpus; Kiknadze; Kevin Tuite (notably on the Kajaveti raid and Samdzimari); Charachidze (1968).

figures.gloss: The demon-crushing hero-god of Pshavi and Khevsureti, wielder of an iron mace, who with his companion Iakhsari cleared the land of the ogre-demons (devi) so that people could settle it; his shrine at Karati remained a resort for the mad and the possessed, whom Kopala alone can free.
figures.facet: A chaoskampf localized to real geography — each slain devi anchors a named place — and prolonged into therapy: the demon-slayer's ancient war continues as exorcism of individual patients.
figures.provenance: Pshav-Khevsur oral corpus; Kiknadze; Tuite on Kopala and Iakhsari; Charachidze (1968).

figures.gloss: The shrine's priestly officer: the khevisberi (in Khevsureti, khutsesi), who sacrifices, blesses, and presides over the festival, flanked by shrine servants (dasturi). Authority runs by inheritance and community recognition within a shrine's serving lineage, not by any church ordination.
figures.facet: A complete indigenous clergy operating in Orthodox Georgia without bishops or ordination — this office runs the shrine's calendar and sacrifice, while revelation itself belongs to the separate oracle, the kadagi.
figures.provenance: Bardavelidze's fieldwork; Charachidze (1968); Tuite on the kadagi institution.

figures.gloss: Sacred beer brewed from communal barley in the shrine's own brewery (salude) — among the most sacred and restricted buildings of the complex — and poured and drunk at the great festivals, above all high-summer Atengenoba (from the feast of St. Athenogenes). The khevisberi consecrates it; sharing it is communion with the jvari.
figures.facet: Where the host church put the chalice, the shrine system put the beer-vat: the sanctified communal drink is the festival's sacramental center, brewed rather than transubstantiated.
figures.provenance: Bardavelidze on shrine breweries and festival cycles; Tuite on Atengenoba in Pshavi and Khevsureti; surviving practice in Tusheti's saludes.

figures.gloss: The herald and steward of Morige Ghmerti, intermediary between the withdrawn high god and the khvtishvilni; invoked in the fixed opening sequence of highland prayer before any other shrine-being is addressed.
figures.facet: He is pure procedure made divine — a god whose entire function is to open the channel of address, ranked before the very powers he serves.
figures.provenance: Georges Charachidze, 'Le systeme religieux de la Georgie paienne' (1968).

figures.gloss: Demon-slaying jvari of Pshavi and Kopala's companion in the campaigns against the devi; like Kopala he was enshrined once those wars cleared the highland valleys for settlement.
figures.facet: He shows the chaoskampf run as a partnership rather than a solo labor — conquest of the demon-haunted wilderness credited to a pair, not a single champion.
figures.provenance: Pshav-Khevsur oral corpus; Kevin Tuite on Kopala and Iakhsari; Charachidze (1968).

figures.gloss: The kaji-woman carried off from Kajaveti in Giorgi's raid, enshrined at Khakhmati as a demon-born power sworn by in women's matters and invoked for childbirth, health, and the dairy herds.
figures.facet: A captured enemy is absorbed rather than destroyed — the raid's plunder becomes a resident goddess, her demon origin folded into the very shrine network that defeated her people.
figures.provenance: Khevsur ballad corpus on the Kajaveti raid; Kevin Tuite on Samdzimari's cult at Khakhmati; Charachidze (1968).

figures.gloss: The great jvari of Pshavi, whose oak sanctuary and summer festival headed the Pshav shrine network; Georgian scholarship links the cult's eponym to King Giorgi IV Lasha (r. 1213-23), the tradition's earliest datable anchor.
figures.facet: A named medieval king lends his title to a shrine power that otherwise behaves exactly like the highlands' other khvtishvilni — history absorbed into the very system used to date it.
figures.provenance: Georges Charachidze (1968); Khevsur-Pshav festival corpus documented by Vazha-Pshavela and Vera Bardavelidze.

figures.gloss: The ubiquitous local guardian goddess venerated at springs, cliffs, and settlements across the highlands as protector of the locale's people, herds, and travelers.
figures.facet: Beneath the named, ranked khvtishvilni sits this unranked, everywhere-goddess — the cosmos's most local layer, tied to no single shrine but to the ground itself.
figures.provenance: Vera Bardavelidze's mountaineer ethnography; Georges Charachidze (1968).

figures.gloss: The Khevsur captive forced to eat serpent flesh, who thereby gains the power to understand the speech of plants and animals; the gift estranges him from human society and, in the best-known telling, fades again before his death.
figures.facet: Wisdom here is a curse of translation — understanding every other voice in creation leaves him unable to be understood by his own people.
figures.provenance: Khevsur oral legend, source of Vazha-Pshavela's poem 'Mindia' (1913-17).

figures.gloss: The oracle whom a jvari or khati seizes in trance and speaks through, announcing the shrine-being's will, adjudicating disputes, and sometimes naming the next officers. Selection runs through possession and dream, not appointment.
figures.facet: The system's revelation channel proper: where the khevisberi administers the shrine's calendar and sacrifice, the kadagi is the mouth through which the god actually speaks.
figures.provenance: Vera Bardavelidze's fieldwork; Georges Charachidze (1968); Kevin Tuite on the kadagi institution.

edges.note: Both continue the medieval Caucasian cult of St. George (Uas Gergi / Giorgi) re-grown into the chief native divine patron — one saint, two neighboring re-paganizations, the names still cognate.

Documented transmission in a word: Ossetic dzuar is the Georgian jvari ('cross') borrowed across the range — and on both slopes the 'cross' names a shrine-being with feast, beer, and sacrifice, not a church.

HOST SEAM: jvari and khati are Orthodoxy's own 'cross' and 'icon' — the shrine divinities wear the host church's vocabulary while refusing its theology of mediation: here the khati does not point to a prototype, it IS the local lord.

The consecrated communal drink at the rite's center — shrine beer standing where the host liturgy sets the eucharistic cup; a structural rhyme under the same nominal religion, not a documented derivation.

Across the main ridge: medieval Georgian churchmen built Tkhaba-Yerdy in Ingushetia (Georgian architectural and epigraphic traits), and on both slopes the same thing followed — nominally Christian shrines re-absorbed into a native mountain cult of feast, sacrifice, and beer.
Intra-tradition: the Orderer and his enfeoffed children — heaven as a medieval Georgian court, the communities of men as the fiefs (saqmo) of divine lords.
Intra-tradition: the two campaigners against the demon-world — George raids Kajaveti, Kopala clears the devi from the valleys; conquest and settlement as two phases of one war.
Kviria is the herald through whom Morige Ghmerti's court is addressed — the opening formula of highland prayer.
The two campaigners who together cleared the devi from the Pshav-Khevsur valleys.
The captured kaji-woman of Giorgi's Kajaveti raid, now a shrine power of her own at Khakhmati.
One of the named khvtishvilni: Pshavi's paramount shrine-lord within the enfeoffed system.
An older, unranked layer of place-guardianship underlying the same feudal shrine cosmos.
Administrative priesthood and revelatory oracle — two offices of one shrine apparatus.
Neighboring Caucasus highland women's cult goddesses of childbirth and household increase, each documented as the last active layer of its system.
The local-place mother-earth guardian, worshipped for the land's fertility and the safety of its people — independent structural parallel, not a documented link.

dossier.essence: The shrine religion of the Georgian mountaineers of Khevsureti, Pshavi, and Tusheti — nominally Orthodox communities whose actual religious life ran through local shrine-lords (jvari, khati), a lay priesthood of khevisberis, and possessed oracles, all operating outside the church. Whether it preserves pre-Christian Georgian religion or is a medieval re-formation of Christianity in the church-less highlands remains the live scholarly dispute.
dossier.cosmos: Morige Ghmerti, God the Orderer, rules from above but is not approached at the shrines; his 'children', the khvtishvilni, are enfeoffed over human communities, each village the saqmo — the vassalage — of its own jvari, heaven mirroring the medieval Georgian court. The land itself is conquered territory: heroes like Kopala and Iakhsari cleared the valleys of demons, and the frontier with the demonic world of devi and kaji persists as a working boundary of purity and danger. The dead are remembered and provisioned at commemorative feasts.
dossier.practice: The khevisberi (khutsesi in Khevsureti) sacrifices, blesses, and rules the festivals, flanked by dasturi shrine servants; the kadagi, seized by the jvari, speaks its will aloud. Offices are filled by possession and dream, not ordination or literacy. The great festivals — above all high-summer Atengenoba — turn on animal sacrifice and beer brewed in the shrine's own sacred brewery (salude), and each jvari's banners, bells, and treasury mark its wealth and standing; strict purity rules, especially around women, police the boundary of the sacred.
dossier.canon: Oral: the Pshav-Khevsur ballad corpus and the shrines' foundation legends (andrezi), recorded by Georgian ethnography from the 19th century onward; Charachidzé (1968) is the great systematic account.
dossier.reflects: Religious capture — the host church's own words (cross, icon, saint) re-purposed as the names of resident gods, showing how a folk system can metabolize an official religion into something structurally its own.
sources: Georges Charachidzé, 'Le système religieux de la Géorgie païenne' (1968)
Kevin Tuite, 'Lightning, Sacrifice, and Possession in the Traditional Religions of the Caucasus' (2004)
Vera Bardavelidze, 'Drevneyshie religioznye verovaniya i obryadovoe graficheskoe iskusstvo gruzinskikh plemen' (1957)

links.cite: Kevin Tuite, 'Lightning, Sacrifice, and Possession in the Traditional Religions of the Caucasus' (2004)
